title: Recursion of Thought: Divide and Conquer Reasoning with Language Models

structured.summary: This paper proposes Recursion of Thought (RoT), a novel, model-agnostic inference framework that enables language models to perform recursive divide-and-conquer reasoning by generating special tokens to control recursion and spawn subproblems in separate contexts. Evaluated on synthetic arithmetic and algorithmic benchmarks with extremely long reasoning chains, RoT significantly outperforms direct answering and chain-of-thought baselines limited by context size, demonstrating strong scaling to problem difficulties requiring hundreds of thousands of intermediate tokens.

strengths.point: Novel recursive divide-and-conquer inference method without architectural changes
strengths.evidence: RoT introduces special tokens (GO, STOP, THINK, TAIL) that allow an LM to recursively split problems into subproblems solved in separate contexts, enabling reasoning beyond fixed context length.

strengths.point: Strong empirical results on very long reasoning chains across model scales
strengths.evidence: RoT achieves near-perfect accuracy on complex synthetic arithmetic and algorithmic tasks on GPT-3 Ada and tiny randomly initialized Transformers and LSTMs, scaling to reasoning chains far exceeding 2048 token context windows.

weaknesses.point: Heavy reliance on supervised ground-truth recursive traces limits practical applicability
weaknesses.evidence: Training requires detailed annotated token-level recursive reasoning traces specifying when to emit special tokens and subproblem answers, which may be infeasible for natural language tasks.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison and positioning relative to prior recursive or hierarchical reasoning methods
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not sufficiently review or experimentally compare RoT to existing recursive neural reasoning frameworks such as neural module networks, stack-based reasoning, or recent recursive prompting approaches.

questions.question: Can RoT be extended to natural language reasoning tasks without access to supervised recursive traces?
questions.motivation: Addressing this would broaden RoT’s applicability beyond synthetic benchmarks and enable use in real-world complex reasoning scenarios.

questions.question: How does error propagation in recursive calls affect final answer correctness and can RoT incorporate error mitigation or recovery mechanisms?
questions.motivation: Understanding robustness is critical for deploying recursive reasoning methods in noisy or ambiguous environments.

critique_points.point_001: Heavy reliance on supervised ground-truth recursive traces limits practical applicability. Training requires detailed annotated token-level recursive reasoning traces specifying when to emit special tokens and subproblem answers, which may be infeasible for natural language tasks
critique_points.point_002: Lack of comparison and positioning relative to prior recursive or hierarchical reasoning methods. The paper does not sufficiently review or experimentally compare RoT to existing recursive neural reasoning frameworks such as neural module networks, stack-based reasoning, or recent recursive prompting approaches
